speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number seven of the interview with Dr. Fritz Gluckstein. You were just telling-- you were just telling us how you met your future wife at the Hillel House at the University of Minnesota. Please continue. Yes. Well, then the Rabbi of the Hillel House married us. And-- but of course, we postponed our marriage until I graduated. And then, of course, I stayed in Minneapolis to await military service. We expected it.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, I went into a veterinary. I graduated from veterinary school. And I went into meat inspection for the army. And I remember something I'll always-- it always served me well. My-- at that time, one night at sea, after the [INAUDIBLE], we listened to news. One night he came on, 'And now the word for today.' And what he said-- James Conant, the then president of Harvard-- he used to say, 'behold the turtle. He makes progress only when he sticks his neck out.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Why do I bring it up? I was in meat inspection and a veterinarian to inspect meat. Many people don't know that all the meat they eat is inspected by veterinarians. They don't realize. All you know, the meat you eat is fit for consumption. It Was inspected. I had meat inspection.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, comes a great man from Washington-- the big boss to look around-- Dr. Stein. Low and behold, Dr. Stein, and the local boss, and some other veterinarians go around and come to my station. At the time, I just was in charge of calves, stool of the calf. Low and behold comes a calf. And I say, immature. Pull it out. It's immature.

speaker: interviewee
text: Immediately, the local boss questions me and said, well why? In my opinion, it isn't. And low and behold, they ask around. He asked one veterinarian who said, no, it's immature. The other said-- one guy-- why, no. I would pass it. And the one guy, I didn't know. Fine. OK. Turns to leave. Now, I should have been quiet, but what did I do? I said, gentlemen, no. Dr. Stein, what is it, is it immature or not immature? And what it is, I put the big boss-- he had to make the final decision. Is it immature? I put him on the spot. He looked at it, it is immature. Well, I stuck my neck out, but I made progress.

speaker: interviewee
text: Months later, I was at special detail at a laboratory in Washington. In fact, I took my physical for the army. I served-- I was called to the army station for duty right here in Alexandria. I stuck my neck out, but I made progress. He just could have said-- not upheld me. And of course, the local boss never forgave me because there he was overruled in front of me. But I-- when I had the nerve to call him back and now make a decision. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said, don't let him get away with all that fuss, and then he let it hang. No. Now let's see here what is going on. That is behold the turtle. He makes progress only if he stick his neck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you did all of your military service in the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was fortunate for-- I toured Chicago, Meat and Dairy Hygiene School. At the last day, one of our last days, personnel chief from the veterinary called with a sign. I walk in there. He addresses me in German. But I said, what-- Ah. He laughed. You are surprised. His name was Murray. That's not a German name. Well, he said, yes. I spoke German very early. My mother was from Hamburg. And I wound up at [INAUDIBLE] in the biological warfare laboratory. And it was risky, but I learned a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you were-- you finished your military service, what sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to agriculture and for the agricultural service. And I got actually interested in information. I always was interested in information. I remember in veterinary school, we felt the library should be open Sundays and nights. And I said, I'll go fetch-- go up and talk to the professor who's in charge of the library. I said, we have to have it open. Of course we have to. You're here by appointed chairman of a committee to come up with a plan to keep it open. And that's the way I get more and scattered information.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you, did you have children in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have my daughter, Ruth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you raise her the way you had been raised, or did you adopt a new American approach to child rearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I adopted the American approach to child rearing. Definitely no. You have to be flexible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is your daughter aware of the-- your experiences during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. Not that I sat down and chronologically told her about it. But she picked it up and asked questions here and there.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's over 50 years now since the end of the war. Do you have some thoughts you'd like to share or a message you'd like to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I've seen-- it was a rough time. But I believe that for every evil person, there is at least one very good person. You mustn't forget that. At least one. Probably two.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a-- a final thought. People sometimes wonder what can we do to make the world a better place to live? Well, I don't think we have to look very far. Just remember Hillel, who said, do not do unto others what you do not want others to do unto you. If you follow that, I think the world will be a much better place to live. Thank you very much for sharing your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you. Next to me is my wife, Maran. Behind me is my daughter, Ruth Roe. Next to her is my son-in-law, David Roe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, I'm the only child of Dr. Fritz Gluckstein. And I just wanted to say that growing up as a child of a survivor, I really take it for granted that he survived. And I think only as an adult am I able to look back and reflect, and it really means something to me. And I'm just glad that I got to this point where I can appreciate it while he's still around.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess what I'd like to say is I have-- members of my family went through the Holocaust as well. They're no longer with us now. But one of the things that I think Fritz helps me with is although we will never know the horrors that he went through, just to think about how good that we have it now with our life and always remember that. As Ruth mentioned, sometimes you take things for granted. And talking with Fritz and thinking about some of the things that he went through helps to put things in perspective. And I always think that's important.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fritz and I were just married in November of 1996 even though I have known him for a number of years. His wife Ethel and I were friends, and he was a friend, too. And the thing that I feel in knowing him both as a friend and now as a spouse is something that his wife, Ethel, said to me, was-- I asked her when she was dying at a very young age of 59, whether she was bitter or not. And she said to me, ever since I met Fritz, my life has been very good. And to me, that is the greatest compliment that one human being can give another. And I know that it's true also, because since I've met him and been with him, my life has been very good. And I think it's a great reflection on him that despite everything he went through, he has come through it as a loving, caring, human being with a great zest for life. Who knows that you grab the moment-- and he really does. And it's a great joy to be with him and be part of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are we looking at here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a document that came together with the iron cross my father was awarded in the First World War. The year is 1918. And it was given the name of His Majesty, the Emperor and King.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This document-- it notifies my father that he was awarded a so-called honor cross for front line soldiers. This was given in 1935, after Hitler had come to power. And it starts right here. 'In the name of the Fuhrer, our leader, and the Reich Chancellor, you're hereby awarded the honor cross.' This happened in 1935, and it didn't matter that my father was a Jew. The law stated that anyone who fought at the front was to receive this cross and therefore, my father received it, too. You can see this swastika right here. The cross says it here. Right there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell us about this document.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to mention that all the documents that will be shown from now on are copies. The original are at the Holocaust Museum. This is the final report card from high school. And it is significant because it states here the reason why this is the final one. It says, 'he leaves the school'-- and I translate-- 'because of the order to dissolution of the Jewish school system.' After that, no more school was open in-- Jewish school-- was permitted in Germany. That happened in June 1942. And what you can see here, the name is-- my name was Fritz Israel. And even if you go down, if you see at the bottom here, it was signed here by the principal, Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the home room teacher, Israel. And if you go down even farther-- my father, of course, had to sign the-- or did he? I don't know. But anyhow, anyone who signed had to use the name Israel. Describe this document please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a certificate of release from a transit camp in the Rosenstrasse. We were scooped up actually from the street, and put in this camp. And I was released because my mother wasn't Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was March of '43 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about these please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a pass giving me permission to use public transportation. It was issued in March '43. And there too you can see the Jew, Fritz Israel Gluckstein. And it gives the address. And it gives me permission to use all means of transportation. And here-- whenever I moved, I had to go to the police station and to change the address. For instance, it says, 'the abode of the Gluckstein is now this street.' And then I moved again. And at the bottom is just an extension. And again, here for the last. Whenever you moved, you simply had to go and have it extended.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this item.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the star which I had to wear right above my heart. It started in 1941. And it had to be firmly attached. In fact, the Gestapo went around with a pencil. And if they could get a pencil behind it, this was very bad. In fact, you don't see it here, but my mother very carefully lined it so that it could be removed from one garment to the other. And each one-- we had to buy those. We had to pay for them and also hand over some clothing coupons.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are we looking at here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a labor document. Whenever you worked for a factory or for a demolition firm, you had to show that. And they entered the date, begin of employment, and end of employment. And also, you could show that you were employed. You can see here on the top through the graph that it was a factory, and below, Richard Wahlisch. That was the firm that did the demolition work. That tells you when you started and what you did-- it was worker-- and then when the employment ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us-- describe what we're looking at here.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was originally an old people's home, the Grosse Hamburger Strasse. This was used as a transit camp, too. And I had to report to this camp close to my 16th birthday. It was 1943. And at that time, the notorious Gestapo agent, Alois Brunner of Vienna, was there. And at that time, the deportation were really increased. And actually, again, they interviewed me. But because of my mother he let me go. The name Alois brings to mind there was another Alois in Berlin, the owner of a restaurant. His real name was Alois Hitler. Of course, he couldn't use the full name. He called his restaurant, Alois. However, at that time, this was '35, '36, '37, I would say. He permitted the Jewish lawyers to meet there once a month to have a social hour. And he did that although he was the half-brother of Adolph Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Move to the top.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the headquarters of Adolf Eichmann. And I worked some time there. And actually, the building is still standing. It used to be an administrative office for some Jewish agency. It was taken over by Eichmann and it is still standing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the address of the headquarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Kurfurstenstrasse. In fact, when you talked about this, you didn't say headquarters. It was Kurfurstenstrasse that everybody knew what that meant.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these pictures appear in a book that we have taken them from. And the book is entitled, 'Stella,' by Peter Wyden. What are we looking at here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a document that excluded me from the labor force. Anyone inducted into the armed forces first had to serve a period of time in the arbeitsdiensts, or labor force. But you can see here, Israel. And at the bottom I had to sign it. It says, Fritz Israel Gluckstein. And then you had to sign Juden-- Jew. And identification, Berlin. And give the number of my identification card. Any time you have to sign a document, you had to do this. Give the number of your identification.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the reason for your exclusion from this requirement?

speaker: interviewee
text: And right here it says the reason was [GERMAN]. I was considered a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please describe what we're looking at here.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a document of exclusion from the Wehrmacht, the armed services of the German army.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this issued to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1944. In fact, on January 24th. On my birthday, on 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you would have been how old then?

speaker: interviewee
text: 17 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are we looking at in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of one of the few synagogues that remained standing after the war. It's a synagogue in the Pestalozzistrasse. And if you see the curtain in front of the arc, this is a curtain that was -- it's a synagogue in which I became bar mitzvah. Some way it survived. The synagogue where I which I'd been bar mitzvah was severely damaged during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And you explained this was like on the eve of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] please describe this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the good times before Hitler came to power. I would say it would be in 1932. I was about 5 years old. And the car belonged to my uncle Ernst, the husband of my aunt Frieda, who was my mother's sister. And just before the eve of a trip we took into the mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin. That was taken in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the last name of your aunt and uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aunt Elfride and Ernst Dressler. And I even can tell you the make of the car. It was a Fiat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my parents, and my aunt, and her husband. Sitting is my father in his typical posture. Whenever you came with a camera, he stopped smiling. He took official posture as a judge. My father's name is Georg Gluckstein. Next to him is my mother, Hedwig Gluckstein, nee Heinrich. Behind my father is my aunt, Elfride Dressler, my mother's sister. And next to her is her husband, Ernst Dressler. It was taken in 1954 in Berlin in my parents apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe this picture, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a graduation picture. I graduate from veterinary school in Saint Paul, Minnesota. The year was 1955.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about these two pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: There is a picture of my parents taken in 1955 in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were living at this point--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Minneapolis, Minnesota. I just had graduated and gotten married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And please describe this picture for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter's wedding picture. You can see me at the left, then my son-in-law David Rowe, my daughter, and my first wife, Ethel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your daughter's name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In August 22nd, 1993.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did your daughter get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Gaithersburg, Maryland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell us about this picture. This is a picture of my first wife, Ethel, Ruth's mother. I would say, without her, you probably wouldn't have had an interview today. I never talked very much about what happened. And through our marriage, occasionally she asked a question here. Never pressed me. And bit by bit, I got to talk about it. And finally, she learned about what actually happened and I was able to talk about it. Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Maran's and my wedding picture. It was taken in Bethesda on November 29th, 1996.
